(French pronunciation: [[ʁasiŋ klœb də lɑ̃s]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French")), commonly referred to as **RC Lens** or simply as **Lens**, is a [French professional football](/wiki/Football_in_France "Football in France") club based in the northern city of [Lens](/wiki/Lens,_Pas-de-Calais "Lens, Pas-de-Calais") in the [department](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France") of [Pas-de-Calais](/wiki/Pas-de-Calais "Pas-de-Calais"). The club's nickname, *Les Sang et Or* (transl. The Blood and Gold), comes from its traditional colours of red and gold. As of the [2024–25 season](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_RC_Lens_season "2024–25 RC Lens season"), Lens competes in [Ligue 1](/wiki/Ligue_1 "Ligue 1"), the highest tier of French football.

**Racing Club de Lens** (French pronunciation: [[ʁasiŋ klœb də lɑ̃s]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French")), commonly referred to as **RC Lens** or simply as **Lens**, is a [French professional football](/wiki/Football_in_France "Football in France") club based in the northern city of [Lens](/wiki/Lens,_Pas-de-Calais "Lens, Pas-de-Calais") in the [department](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France") of [Pas-de-Calais](/wiki/Pas-de-Calais "Pas-de-Calais"). The club's nickname, *Les Sang et Or* (transl. The Blood and Gold), comes from its traditional colours of red and gold. As of the [2024–25 season](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_RC_Lens_season "2024–25 RC Lens season"), Lens competes in [Ligue 1](/wiki/Ligue_1 "Ligue 1"), the highest tier of French football.

Lens won the [Division 1](/wiki/Ligue_1 "Ligue 1") (now Ligue 1) title in the [1997–98 season](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_French_Division_1 "1997–98 French Division 1"), and has finished as runner-up on five occasions, most recently in the [2022–23](/wiki/2022-23_Ligue_1 "2022-23 Ligue 1") season. The club has a strong rivalry with neighbors [Lille OSC](/wiki/Lille_OSC "Lille OSC"), with whom they contest the *[Derby du Nord](/wiki/Derby_du_Nord "Derby du Nord")*.

## History

### Origin of the club

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=RC_Lens&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Origin of the club")]

The club was established in 1906 in [Lens](/wiki/Lens,_Pas-de-Calais "Lens, Pas-de-Calais") by local students who enjoyed playing [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") on the city's Place Verte (the current Place de la République). The name "Racing Club de Lens" was chosen as a reference to [Racing Club de Roubaix](/wiki/Racing_Club_de_Roubaix "Racing Club de Roubaix") and [Racing Club de France](/wiki/Racing_Club_de_Paris "Racing Club de Paris"), both popular clubs at the time.

The club's first board of directors was formed by the parents of those students. The club originally played in green and black to represent the founding location; they wore green to represent the name of the home pitch, "Verte", (meaning 'green' in French), and black to represent the omnipresence of the [coal mining](/wiki/Coal_mining "Coal mining") industry in the surrounding area.

Between 1907 and 1912, the players were forced to change home grounds twice before settling at the Parc des Glissoires, situated between [Avion](/wiki/Avion,_Pas-de-Calais "Avion, Pas-de-Calais") and [Lens](/wiki/Lens,_Pas-de-Calais "Lens, Pas-de-Calais").

During [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), in common with all French sports clubs, the team's activities were stopped and did not restart until 1919. By this time, Lens had changed their playing colours to sky blue.

### Of blood and gold

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=RC_Lens&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Of blood and gold")]

It was in 1924 that the red and gold colours first appeared. One legend says that Pierre Moglia, president of the club from 1923 to 1930, chose the colours of the [Spanish flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Spain "Flag of Spain") after a colleague from the club remarked that the Saint-Léger church ruins, which they had walked past one evening, were the last visible remains of the local [Spanish domination](/wiki/Spanish_Netherlands "Spanish Netherlands") in [1648](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War"). Some people also say that the colours were derived as a reference to the local coal mines: the red for the blood of the miners and the gold for the coal which was valuable at the time.

It was also in 1924 that the club was authorised to play at the newly built municipal stadium Raoul Briquet (nowadays Léo Lagrange). The first match for the club in their new colours was played for the inauguration of the stadium.

In 1926, British footballer Kid Fenton was the first star who played for Lens. He stayed for eight seasons and became a strong favourite with the club's supporters. This was also the year the first supporters group was formed, and also saw the first occasion that Lens captured the Championnat d'Artois.

In 1929, Lens won the North championship and won promotion for the first time to the Division d'Honneur of the Ligue du Nord with the clubs [Olympique Lillois](/wiki/Lille_OSC "Lille OSC"), [RC Roubaix](/wiki/RC_Roubaix "RC Roubaix"), [Excelsior Athlétic Club de Roubaix](/wiki/Excelsior_Athl%C3%A9tic_Club_de_Roubaix "Excelsior Athlétic Club de Roubaix") and AC Amiens.

In the Artois League, the club steadily gained prestige, and in 1932 the club inaugurated the [Stade Félix Bollaert](/wiki/Stade_F%C3%A9lix_Bollaert "Stade Félix Bollaert").

### The first victories

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=RC_Lens&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: The first victories")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/RCLens3738.jpg/250px-RCLens3738.jpg)](/wiki/File:RCLens3738.jpg)

RC Lens squad during season 1937/1938

In 1937, Lens gained access to the [first division](/wiki/Ligue_1 "Ligue 1") after finishing first in the [second division](/wiki/Ligue_2 "Ligue 2"), led by such players as [Stefan Dembicki](/wiki/Stefan_Dembicki "Stefan Dembicki") and Spechtl. Lens even managed to reach the last 16 of the [Coupe de France](/wiki/Coupe_de_France "Coupe de France"), although the team was eventually eliminated by the [Red Star](/wiki/Red_Star_F.C. "Red Star F.C."), 3–2.

In 1943, Lens won the first division of the Northern Zone thanks to Dembicki, who scored 43 goals in 30 games. A year earlier, in a Coupe de France match, he had scored 17 goals, which still remains to this day the world record for goals scored by one player in a single competitive match.

Immediately following [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Lens finished in sixth place in the 1945–46 season, but they were relegated the following year. In 1948, the club played its first Coupe de France final, which they lost 3–2 against Lille. A year later, Lens was promoted to the first division, and [Maryan Wisnieski](/wiki/Maryan_Wisnieski "Maryan Wisnieski") was recruited, in 1953. Problems with the board, however, made him quit the club; he joined Italian club [UC Sampdoria Genoa](/wiki/U.C._Sampdoria "U.C. Sampdoria"), though without much success.

In 1962, the city of Lens' mines were shut down and the future of the club was at stake, given that most of the players were miners by profession. Between 1956 and 1968, survival was hard. Nevertheless, in 1964, Lens finished third, with [Ahmed Oudjani](/wiki/Ahmed_Oudjani "Ahmed Oudjani") the top scorer with 30 goals. Another famous player, [Georges Lech](/wiki/Georges_Lech "Georges Lech"), joined Lens, although the club was relegated in 1968. The following year, the mine's administrators rescinded their ownership of Lens, which signalled the end of professional football at the [Stade Bollaert-Delelis](/wiki/Stade_Bollaert-Delelis "Stade Bollaert-Delelis"). Lens was now once again an amateur club, one year after its relegation, and the long-term future looked very bleak.

### The good years and the fall

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=RC_Lens&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: The good years and the fall")]

However, better days arrived in 1960 after the town council took interest in Racing Club de Lens. Lens' mayor, [André Delelis](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Delelis "André Delelis"), was a long-term supporter and recognized the importance of the club's success to the overall morale of the city. With the future president, Jean Bondoux, the mayor brought together volunteers and subscriptions to help the club survive. Moreover, the city recovered ownership of the stadium from the closing mine industry.

The following twenty years saw a slow but steady improvement in the club's fortunes. In 1972, Lens reached the semi-finals of the Coupe de France, and the arrival of two Polish players helped the club win promotion to the first division. In 1975, Lens once again reached the final of the Coupe de France against the powerful [Saint-Étienne](/wiki/AS_Saint-%C3%89tienne "AS Saint-Étienne"), but lost the game 0–2.

As finalist of the Coupe de France, Lens had the opportunity to participate in its first [UEFA Cup Winners' Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "UEFA Cup Winners' Cup"), but the team was knocked out quickly by the Dutch club [ADO Den Haag](/wiki/ADO_Den_Haag "ADO Den Haag").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Stade_Felix-Bollaert.jpg/250px-Stade_Felix-Bollaert.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stade_Felix-Bollaert.jpg)

[Stade Félix Bollaert](/wiki/Stade_F%C3%A9lix_Bollaert "Stade Félix Bollaert")

Lens' steady progress continued, and after finishing second in the league behind [Nantes](/wiki/FC_Nantes "FC Nantes"), they managed to qualify to the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League"). They knocked out Swedish club [Malmö FF](/wiki/Malm%C3%B6_FF "Malmö FF"), and more impressively, the strong Italian club [Lazio](/wiki/S.S._Lazio "S.S. Lazio"). After an away defeat (2–0), they won 6–0 at the [Stade Bollaert-Delelis](/wiki/Stade_Bollaert-Delelis "Stade Bollaert-Delelis") after extra-time. Unfortunately, after this rare international exploit for a French club, they were then eliminated by [East German](/wiki/East_German "East German") side [1. FC Magdeburg](/wiki/1._FC_Magdeburg "1. FC Magdeburg"). Worse still, the club went back to the second division in 1978.

The step backwards was very brief and immediate promotion back to the French elite level was achieved in 1979 with [Roger Lemerre](/wiki/Roger_Lemerre "Roger Lemerre") as head coach. During the 1980s, [Gérard Houllier](/wiki/G%C3%A9rard_Houllier "Gérard Houllier") and [Joachim Marx](/wiki/Joachim_Marx "Joachim Marx") succeeded him. These were great gains to the team, even though the club lost important players such as [Didier Sénac](/wiki/Didier_S%C3%A9nac "Didier Sénac"), [Gaëtan Huard](/wiki/Ga%C3%ABtan_Huard "Gaëtan Huard") and [Philippe Vercruysse](/wiki/Philippe_Vercruysse "Philippe Vercruysse").

### Martel's takeover

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=RC_Lens&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Martel's takeover")]

In August 1988 [Gervais Martel](/wiki/Gervais_Martel "Gervais Martel"), a wealthy local businessman, bought control of the club, with the help of Serge Doré. During the same year, [Arnaud Dos Santos](/wiki/Arnaud_Dos_Santos "Arnaud Dos Santos") was named head coach of the club, and led the club back to the first division in 1991.

In 1993 and 1994, Lens' strongest team to that date were highly competitive at the top of the league, and the team qualified for the UEFA Cup twice in a row. Lens also reached the semi-final of the Coupe de France after knocking out [Paris Saint-Germain](/wiki/Paris_Saint-Germain_F.C. "Paris Saint-Germain F.C.") at the [Parc des Princes](/wiki/Parc_des_Princes "Parc des Princes"), although the team lost to [Montpellier](/wiki/Montpellier_HSC "Montpellier HSC").

In [1998](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_RC_Lens_season "1997–98 RC Lens season"), *les Sang et Or* wrote the best page of their history under [Daniel Leclercq](/wiki/Daniel_Leclercq "Daniel Leclercq") ("the Druid"): French champions, [Coupe de la Ligue](/wiki/Coupe_de_la_Ligue "Coupe de la Ligue") semi-finalists and finalists of the Coupe de France (against PSG, a 2–1 defeat). Like a symbol, it is a player who started his career in Lens, [Yohan Lachor](/wiki/Yohan_Lachor "Yohan Lachor"), who scored the goal in [Auxerre](/wiki/AJ_Auxerre "AJ Auxerre") giving Lens the title in front of [Metz](/wiki/FC_Metz "FC Metz"). Under the "Druid", Lens won its second major title in 1999 with the Coupe de la Ligue against Metz, with a goal from [Daniel Moreira](/wiki/Daniel_Moreira "Daniel Moreira"). That year, in the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League"), Lens also became the only club to have beaten English team [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") at the famous [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium_(1923) "Wembley Stadium (1923)") (1–0, with a goal from [Mickaël Debève](/wiki/Micka%C3%ABl_Deb%C3%A8ve "Mickaël Debève")), although they were knocked out on aggregate score over two matches.

During the next season, Leclercq was fired, but Lens nonetheless did well to reach the semi-finals of the UEFA Cup. François Brisson's men were eventually eliminated by Arsenal, after they won against [1. FC Kaiserslautern](/wiki/1._FC_Kaiserslautern "1. FC Kaiserslautern") (a 4–1 win in Germany), [Atlético Madrid](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Madrid "Atlético Madrid") and [Celta de Vigo](/wiki/Celta_de_Vigo "Celta de Vigo").

In the 2001–02 season, [Joël Muller](/wiki/Jo%C3%ABl_Muller "Joël Muller") was named head coach. Lens finished second that season and qualified for its second Champions League campaign. The club, however, finished in eighth for the next two years. Muller was replaced during his fourth season by [Francis Gillot](/wiki/Francis_Gillot "Francis Gillot"), who managed to qualify Lens for the [UEFA Intertoto Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Intertoto_Cup "UEFA Intertoto Cup"), which Lens won, ensuring qualification for the UEFA Cup.

During the [2006–07 season](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_Ligue_1 "2006–07 Ligue 1"), the *Sang et Or* finished the first part of the season in second, behind [Lyon](/wiki/Olympique_Lyonnais "Olympique Lyonnais"). But due to a chaotic second half, however, they only finished fifth. A few days later, Francis Gillot resigned.

On 5 June 2007, [Guy Roux](/wiki/Guy_Roux "Guy Roux") made his comeback, although it only lasted three months: He resigned after a 2–1 defeat at [Strasbourg](/wiki/RC_Strasbourg_Alsace "RC Strasbourg Alsace"). [Jean-Pierre Papin](/wiki/Jean-Pierre_Papin "Jean-Pierre Papin") took over, but Lens could not make up any ground throughout the season, finishing 18th, two points behind [Toulouse](/wiki/Toulouse_FC "Toulouse FC"), resulting in relegation to Ligue 2 for the next season. Lens finished the season with just 40 points, winning only 9 times in 38 matches.

After a slow start in their only year in [Ligue 2](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Ligue_2 "2008–09 Ligue 2"), they managed to finish as leaders during the first half of the season. Earning 13 out of 15 points in their first five games of the second half, everything looked set for a quick return to the first league. After only taking five points of the next six games, however, the promotion race was open again, although Lens recovered and became champions, securing promotion to Ligue 1 for [2009–10](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_Ligue_1 "2009–10 Ligue 1"). After the [2010–11 season](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Ligue_1 "2010–11 Ligue 1"), however, they again dropped to Ligue 2.

On 16 May 2014, Lens sealed promotion back to Ligue 1 on the final day of the [season](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_Ligue_2 "2013–14 Ligue 2") following a 2–0 win at [Bastia](/wiki/CA_Bastia "CA Bastia"). On 27 June, however, the League's National Directorate of Management Control (DNCG) blocked Lens' promotion to the top flight due to irregularities in the club's proposed budget for its next season. The issue was a €10 million payment due from major shareholder [Hafiz Mammadov](/wiki/Hafiz_Mammadov "Hafiz Mammadov") that was missing from the accounts. Lens president [Gervais Martel](/wiki/Gervais_Martel "Gervais Martel") claimed a public holiday in Mammadov's native Azerbaijan had resulted in the delay and said the club would appeal. On 15 July, however, their promotion was in jeopardy after an appeal commission upheld their appeal since the missing funds still had not yet arrived in the club's accounts. Lens immediately declared their intention to appeal to the [French Olympic Committee (CNOSF)](/wiki/French_National_Olympic_and_Sports_Committee "French National Olympic and Sports Committee"), which has the power to overrule the DNCG. On 25 July, the CNSOF recommended Lens should be allowed to play in Ligue 1. Because the Stade Bollaert-Delelis was being renovated for [UEFA Euro 2016](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016 "UEFA Euro 2016"), Lens played their home matches for the [2014–15 Ligue 1](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_Ligue_1 "2014–15 Ligue 1") season at the [Stade de la Licorne](/wiki/Stade_de_la_Licorne "Stade de la Licorne"), home of [Amiens](/wiki/Amiens_SC "Amiens SC"), and at the [Stade de France](/wiki/Stade_de_France "Stade de France") in [Saint-Denis](/wiki/Saint-Denis,_Seine-Saint-Denis "Saint-Denis, Seine-Saint-Denis").

It was announced on 29 January 2015 that Lens' promotion from Ligue 2 at the end of the 2013–14 season has been ruled invalid, and will thus be automatically relegated to Ligue 2 for the [2015–16 season](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Ligue_2 "2015–16 Ligue 2"), regardless of where the team places. Thus, in August 2015 Lens returned to Ligue 2, albeit playing at the renovated Stade Bollaert-Delelis. They drew an average home attendance of 28,996 in the 2016–17 season, the highest in Ligue 2 but missed promotion to the Ligue 1 during a tumultuous last day of the season.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

### New direction and return to Ligue 1

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=RC_Lens&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: New direction and return to Ligue 1")]

In the [2017–18 Ligue 2](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Ligue_2 "2017–18 Ligue 2") season, Lens lost their first seven matches in a row, the worst start to a season in the club's history. On 18 September, Lens finally got their first win of the season over [US Quevilly-Rouen](/wiki/US_Quevilly-Rouen "US Quevilly-Rouen") 2–0.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

In the [2018–19 Ligue 2](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_Ligue_2 "2018–19 Ligue 2") season, Lens finished 5th on the table and reached the promotion play off final against [Dijon FCO](/wiki/Dijon_FCO "Dijon FCO"). After a 1–1 draw in the first leg, Lens would lose the second leg and the tie 3–1 after two goalkeeping blunders by [Jérémy Vachoux](/wiki/J%C3%A9r%C3%A9my_Vachoux "Jérémy Vachoux") cost his side a chance of promotion to [Ligue 1](/wiki/Ligue_1 "Ligue 1").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

On 30 April 2020, Lens were promoted to [Ligue 1](/wiki/Ligue_1 "Ligue 1") after the LFP decided to end the seasons of both Ligue 1 and [Ligue 2](/wiki/Ligue_2 "Ligue 2") early due to the [COVID-19 pandemic in France](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_France "COVID-19 pandemic in France"). Lens were second on the table in Ligue 2 at the time of the decision.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In the [2020–21 season](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_Ligue_1 "2020–21 Ligue 1"), Lens finished 7th on the [Ligue 1](/wiki/Ligue_1 "Ligue 1") table after an impressive first season back in France's top flight.[[7]](#cite_note-7) They finished seventh again in the [following season](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_Ligue_1 "2021–22 Ligue 1") with 62 points,[[8]](#cite_note-8) despite being ranked second behind [Paris Saint-Germain](/wiki/Paris_Saint-Germain_F.C. "Paris Saint-Germain F.C.") in the first half of the season.

In the [2022–23 season](/wiki/2022-23_Ligue_1 "2022-23 Ligue 1"), Lens won 10 games out of 10 at home during the first part of the season. On 1 January 2023, Lens beat PSG 3–1 and became the first team to defeat PSG, which had been unbeaten since the start of the season.[[9]](#cite_note-9) On 27 May, Lens won 3–0 against [Ajaccio](/wiki/AC_Ajaccio "AC Ajaccio"), finished 2nd in [Ligue 1](/wiki/2022-23_Ligue_1 "2022-23 Ligue 1") just one point behind PSG. The team qualified for the [Champions League](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_UEFA_Champions_League "2023–24 UEFA Champions League") for the third time in their history after 1998–99 and 2002–03.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The following two seasons - 2023-24 and 2024-5 - were disappointing for the Lens supporters, as the team finished 7th and 8th in the league, respectively. In June 2024, Will Still replaced Franck Haise as head coach.[[11]](#cite_note-11) In June 2025, [Pierre Sage](/wiki/Pierre_Sage "Pierre Sage") took over as head coach.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The [2025-2026 Ligue 1](/wiki/2025%E2%80%9326_Ligue_1 "2025–26 Ligue 1") season saw some amazing performances from Lens, as they were able to challenge PSG for the league title. As of the beginning of April 2026, Lens was only one point behind PSG in the league.

## Former players

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=RC_Lens&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Former players")]

Three Lens players won the gold medal in the [1984 Los Angeles Olympic Games](/wiki/1984_Los_Angeles_Olympic_Games "1984 Los Angeles Olympic Games"): defender [Didier Sénac](/wiki/Didier_S%C3%A9nac "Didier Sénac"), as well as strikers [François Brisson](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Brisson "François Brisson") and [Daniel Xuereb](/wiki/Daniel_Xuereb "Daniel Xuereb") who scored a goal apiece in France's triumph over Brazil 2–0 in the final at the [Pasadena](/wiki/Pasadena,_California "Pasadena, California") Rose Bowl in front of a crowd of 103,000.

For a complete list of RC Lens players, see [Category:RC Lens players](/wiki/Category:RC_Lens_players "Category:RC Lens players")
